coredata.dc:title: Deciphering the mechanism of undoped and heteroatom doped-carbon dots for detection of lead ions at nanomolar level
coredata.dc:creator: Sohal, Neeraj
Basu, Soumen
Maity, Banibrata
coredata.dc:description: Herein, two types of facile, simple, and eco-friendly nanosensors were developed for the sensing of Pb2+ ions follow the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) and dynamic quenching mechanism. Two types of carbon dots i.e., undoped carbon dots (u-Cdots) and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-Cdots) were developed by a rapid single-step approach under high-temperature treatment without any solvent. Both the prepared carbon dots showed high water solubility and excellent stability. The particle size of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots were determined to be 7.5 nm and N, S-Cdots 1.8 nm, respectively which was examined through transmission electron microscope (TEM). The incorporation of heteroatom doping into the N, S-Cdots was examined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The u-Cdots shows excitation-dependent PL behaviour whereas, N, S-Cdots shows excitation-independent PL behaviour. Further, both the carbon dots act as sensitive and selective sensing platforms for Pb2+ ions in nanomolar range. Different photophysical investigations (fluorescence quantum yield, average lifetime, and radiative and non-radiative deactivation channels) were thoroughly studied to support the different PL mechanisms. Both the prepared sensing platforms exhibited a good limit of detection (LOD) i.e., 30 nM and 7 nM for u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots, respectively. The developed sensing platforms were also used in real sample analysis for the detection of Pb2+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced emission enhancement
Dynamic quenching
Lead ions
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots towards Pb2+ ions
Detection of Pb2+ ions in real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences
SRM Institute of Science and Technology
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Neeraj Sohal is grateful to CSIR, India (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/ EMR-II) for a fellowship and funding. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. The authors acknowledge IIT, Roorkee for the XPS study and also for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis.

Neeraj Sohal is grateful to CSIR, India (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/ EMR-II) for a fellowship and funding. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. The authors acknowledge IIT, Roorkee for the XPS study and also for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of u-Cdots (a, b), N, S-Cdots (d, f), inset image of (b) and (e) represents the HRTEM images showing lattice spacing of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots, respectively, and histogram size distribution of u-Cdots (c) and N, S-Cdots (f).

(a) XPS survey spectrum of u-Cdots with a deconvoluted spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) XPS survey spectrum of N, S-Cdots along with a deconvoluted spectrum of (e) N1s, (f) C1s, (g) S2p, (h) O1s, and (i) FT-IR spectra of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots.

Selectivity bar graph of u-Cdots (a) and N, S-Cdots (b) for the detection of different metal ions, (c) PL enhancement (“Turn-on”) graph of u-Cdots in the presence of different concentrations of Pb2+ ions, and (d) Relation between the PL responses (F-F0/F0) of u-Cdots and different concentrations of Pb2+ ions with inset image of linear calibration curve for the determination of Pb2+ ions.

(a) PL quenching (“Turn-off”) graph of N, S-Cdots with the addition of different concentrations of Pb2+ ions, and (b) Relation among the PL responses (F-F0/F0) of N, S-Cdots and various concentrations of Pb2+ ions along with inset image of linear calibration plot for the sensing of Pb2+ ions, (c) TEM image of u-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions, (d) Fluorescence lifetime decays of N, S-Cdots, (e) TEM image of N, S-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions, and (f) Fluorescence lifetime decays of u-Cdots.
The fluorescent probes based on different methods for the detection of Pb2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Deciphering the mechanism of undoped and heteroatom doped-carbon dots for detection of lead ions at nanomolar level

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology Patiala 147004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Thapar Institute of Engineering and Technology Patiala 147004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, two types of facile, simple, and eco-friendly nanosensors were developed for the sensing of Pb2+ ions follow the aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) and dynamic quenching mechanism. Two types of carbon dots i.e., undoped carbon dots (u-Cdots) and nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-Cdots) were developed by a rapid single-step approach under high-temperature treatment without any solvent. Both the prepared carbon dots showed high water solubility and excellent stability. The particle size of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots were determined to be 7.5 nm and N, S-Cdots 1.8 nm, respectively which was examined through transmission electron microscope (TEM). The incorporation of heteroatom doping into the N, S-Cdots was examined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The u-Cdots shows excitation-dependent PL behaviour whereas, N, S-Cdots shows excitation-independent PL behaviour. Further, both the carbon dots act as sensitive and selective sensing platforms for Pb2+ ions in nanomolar range. Different photophysical investigations (fluorescence quantum yield, average lifetime, and radiative and non-radiative deactivation channels) were thoroughly studied to support the different PL mechanisms. Both the prepared sensing platforms exhibited a good limit of detection (LOD) i.e., 30 nM and 7 nM for u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots, respectively. The developed sensing platforms were also used in real sample analysis for the detection of Pb2+ ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Trace metals are natural elements present in the Earth’s crust. In the past decades, the different metal ions were expelled into the water bodies attributed to fast industrialization like metallurgy, electronic industry, and electroplating [1]. Heavy metal ions can be supplemented in the different living organisms via the food chain. They are non-degradable and bio-incompatible which can cause harm to plants, humans, and animals [2]. Lead is called a neurotoxic metal and is found in industries, water, and smoking [3]. Pb2+ poisoning is responsible for various harmful effects on the different parts of the human body [4]. Furthermore, Pb2+ poisoning occurs when the amount of Pb2+ ions is more than 5 µM in the blood [5]. It can become a reason for various human illnesses such as sore muscles, anaemia, headache, memory loss, fatigue, cardiovascular dysfunction, and irritation. Pb2+ poisoning can put adverse effects on pregnant women due to premature birth, stillbirth, miscarriage, and low birth weight. According to the World Health Organization (2006), the maximum acceptable amount of Pb2+ ions in drinking water is 0.01 mg/L. Therefore, the ultrasensitive and selective sensing of Pb2+ ions become an active research area. There have been various methods reported for the sensing of Pb2+ ions like atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), anodic stripping voltammetry, colorimetric, plasma atomic emission spectroscopy, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, and electrochemical techniques [6]. These techniques have a long analysis process, high cost with sophisticated instruments, and complex sample pre-treatment. So, there is a need to design a rapid, facile, and inexpensive system for the detection of Pb2+ ions. Among various techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy has attracted great attention due to excellent features such as simple method, facile, cost-effective, good selectivity, and sensitivity [7,8].

In recent times, Carbon dots (Cdots) are efficient materials having a size less than 10 nm with attractiveproperties like small size, good photophysical properties, good biocompatibility, and less toxic nature [9–11]. These properties enhance their potential in various fields such as sensing, bioimaging, bio-medical application, electrochemical analysis, andoptoelectronic devices [12,13]. There are mainly-two approaches for the synthesis of Cdots i.e., the “top-down approach” in which exfoliation of large carbon structure and the “bottom-up approach” in which carbonization of small organic molecules [14,15]. There were various methods reported for the preparation of Cdots for example arc discharge, electrochemical, laser ablation, microwave, and hydrothermal treatment [16–18]. Recently, the carbonization of carbon precursors at high temperatures is an easy, facile, cost-effective, and less time-consuming method. Cdots exhibit exceptional optical, chemical, and electrical properties, which can be further modified through heteroatom doping by improving their chemical and electronic structure [19,20]. Nitrogen (N) doping into the structure of Cdots makes them electron-donor by altering their electronic structure [21]. Though doping of sulfur provides emissive traps to the photoexcited electrons which lead to modification of the fluorescence towards a higher wavelength. According to the diagonal rule, sulfur shows some similar properties to the nitrogen atoms which is further considered a feasible doping atom with nitrogen atoms [21]. N, S co-doping offers many active sites as well as the synergistic effect between N and S which result in radiative recombination of the trapped electron-holes pairs [22,23]. Combination of nitrogen, sulphur in the structure of Cdots increase the surface passivation result in the removal of non-radiative recombination and increases the fluorescence quantum yield [24].

Photoluminescence (PL) quenching and recovery of Cdots can be used for the determination of several analytes. There are different quenching mechanisms including static quenching, dynamic quenching, FÖrster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [25]. Aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) was observed in photoluminescent organic molecules which oppose the quenching mechanism caused by aggregation, which was first proposed by Tang and co-workers [26]. These effects arise due to restricted intramolecular rotations, vibrations, or motions by aggregation [27]. Also, AIEE molecules are very sensitive to the variation in their surrounding environment like pH and solvent, due to which it can be mostly used for sensing applications. Xu et al. [28] prepared a fluorescent platform based on AIEE of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped Cdots for sensitive determination of Hg(II). Wang et al. [29] designed glutathione modified Cdots (GSH-Cdots) for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions based on the AIEE mechanism.

Our work is mainly focused on the synthesis of two types carbon dots, without heteroatom doping and with heteroatom doping. Both the carbon dots have same application i.e., selective detection of Pb2+ ions but detection mechanism is based on two different mechanisms. We have prepared two kinds of carbon dots i.e., undoped carbon dots (u-Cdots) and nitrogen, sulfur-doped Cdots (N, S-Cdots) by high-temperature treatment. The size and composition of both carbon dots were analyzed by TEM and XPS analysis. The different mechanism for detection of Pb2+ ions were investigated by UV–vis absorption studies, photoluminescence studies, and lifetime decay studies. The comparative selectivity and sensitivity studies for Pb2+ ions sensing was done for both sensors. Both sensors were used further for the sensing of Pb2+ ions in the real sample.

Citric acid (CA) and cysteine (cys) (99 % purity) were purchased from Loba Chemie, India. These were utilized for the synthesis of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots. All chemicals utilized for the preparation of the stock solution of different metal ions like Pb(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, Cr(NO3)3, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)2, Bi(NO3)3, Mg(NO3)2, and Na(NO3) were bought from Loba Chemie, India. Ultrapure deionized (DI) water was utilized for the preparing all stock solutions of metal ions.

Two types of Cdots were developed from CA as a carbon precursor and cysteine as nitrogen, sulfur precursor. The u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots were prepared from CA (2 g) only and CA (2 g) along with cysteine (200 mg) in the absence of any solvent and was treated at 200 °C for 5 min under air conditions, respectively. The above mixture gets converted into a liquid state and turns yellow from colorless and changes into orange color, which confirms the formation of Cdots. Further, the prepared liquid was dissolved in DI water (5 ml) and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. to eliminate the large particles and unreacted product. Then, the collected supernatant was dialyzed by using dialysis bag with 3500 Da molecular weight for 48 h and obtained solution was used as stock solution for further studies.

UV–vis absorption spectra of developed Cdots were observed by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2600). PL emission spectra were recorded through a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, RF-6000). The size, shape, and morphology of Cdots were investigated through (TEM, TALOS F200S G2). Infrared (IR) spectra were also estimated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Shimadzu, IRTracer-100). The chemical state of prepared Cdots was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI 5000 VersaProbe III). Time resolved fluorescence studies were achieved through time-correlated single photon counting via a TCSPC spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon IBH) by using excitation laser source at 375 nm.

The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of prepared Cdots were evaluated w.r.t., quinine sulfate [30] (reference) with ΦR = 0.546 by equation (1):
(1)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
×
A S
A R
×
( A b s )
R
( A b s )
S
×
η
S
2
η
R
2

The subscripts “S” and “R” are symbolized as the sample solution i.e., prepared Cdots, and reference solution i.e., quinine sulfate, respectively. “Abs” indicates absorbance, “A” indicates fluorescence emission area, and “η” indicates refractive index.

The preparation of Cdots was dependent upon the condensation of CA followed by carbonization. The shape and size of the developed Cdots were examined via TEM images. Fig. 1 (a, b) and (d, e) show the TEM images of the u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots, respectively. These TEM images show that particles are spherical and with high uniform dispersity. The inset image of Fig. 1(b) and 1(e) correspond to HRTEM images of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots which are showing lattice fringes with d-spacing value of 0.221 nm and 0.224 nm, respectively. The lattice d-spacing values of both prepared carbon dots corresponds to lattice plane (100) of graphitic carbon. Fig. 1c displays the histogram size distribution of u-Cdots with a particle size ranging with 6.5–11.5 nm and owing to an approximate diameter i.e., 7.5 nm. Similarly, Fig. 1(f) represents the histogram size distribution of N, S-Cdots having a size of particles ranging from 1 to 3 nm with an approximate diameter i.e., 1.8 nm.

The surface composition as well as chemical state of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots were examined through XPS study. The XPS survey spectrum of the u-Cdots displayed main peaks at 530.9 and 284.9 eV related to O1s and C1s, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) [31]. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum of u-Cdots displayed three significant peaks at about 286.9, 283.5, and 282.8 eV attributed to the COC, C-OH, and CC/CC, respectively (Fig. 2(b)) [32,33]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s showed two major peaks at 531.2 and 529.7 eV attributed to the existence of CO and COH, respectively (Fig. 2(c)) [31,34]. The elemental analysis of u-Cdots represents the percentage of atomic concentration of individual element that are C (67.96 %) and O (32.04 %).

Similarly, the XPS survey spectrum of the N, S-Cdots showed four significant peaks at around 530.3, 398.9, 283.8, and 161.8 eV related to O1s, N1s, C1s, and S2p, respectively (Fig. 2(d)) [31,35,36]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum exhibited a dual peak at 398.8 and 398.3 eV signifying the presence of pyridinic N and CN (Fig. 2(e)) [37,38]. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum of N, S-Cdots revealed three major peaks at around 286.7, 284.0, and 282.8 eV refer to the COC, C-OH, and CC/CC, respectively (Fig. 2(f)) [33,39]. After the deconvolution of the S2p spectrum, dual peak was observed at 162.6 and 161.4 eV corresponds to the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 (Fig. 2(g)) [36,40]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2(h)) consist of dual peak at 531.2 and 529.8 eV correspond to the existence of CO and C-OH, respectively [31,34]. The elemental analysis of synthesized N, S-Cdots represents the percentage of atomic concentration of individual element that are C (65.62 %), N (7.57 %), O (22.71 %), and S (4.10 %). The study of different functional groups was investigated through a FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2i) of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots exhibit a broad absorption band at 3400 cm−1 confirming the existence of the OH group. The peaks around 1720, 1630, 1405, and 1210 cm−1 related to CO, CC, CN, and OCO stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2i) [41]. The medium peak around 2550 cm−1 denotes SH stretching vibrations [42]. The different surface functional groups developed on Cdots increase their solubility in water.

Fig. S1(a) and S1(b) show the UV–visible absorption spectra of developed u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots, respectively. UV–visible spectra of prepared u-Cdots signify-two significant peaks at about 290 nm and 340 nm corresponding to π-π* transition due to sp2 aromatic π-domains and n-π* transition due to the CO, respectively. Similarly, the absorption spectra of N, S-Cdots denote two more significant peaks around 245 nm and 345 nm attributing to π-π* transition due to the existence of sp2 aromatic π-domains and n-π* transition because of the CO, respectively. Heteroatom doping in the structure of Cdots modifies its electrical properties by varying the band-gap and optical absorption [21]. Therefore, the prepared N, S-Cdots showed enhancement in the optical absorption as shown in Fig. S1(b).

There are major two kinds of PL emission which are intrinsic recombination attributed to carbon core (band-gap) and extrinsic recombination attributed to the surface defects [43]. The change in the energy band-gap depends upon the size distribution of Cdots related to the quantum confinement effect (QCE) which leads to the excitation-dependent PL emission behavior. On the other hand, the excitation-independent PL emission was observed due to large size distributions of Cdots [44]. This demonstrated that the size is not only responsible for the excitation-dependent PL emission. Therefore, the excitation-dependent and excitation-independent PL emission depend upon various factors such as QCE, surface defects, conjugated π-domains, and zig-zag edges sites [45]. It was reported in the literature [46,47] that the multi-mode absorptions were achieved for less surface passivation of Cdots because of different energy states development and that became reason for their excitation-dependent PL emission. Furthermore, the excitation-independent PL emission was observed in case of high surface passivation of Cdots due to generation of the single transition mode. Enhancement in the surface passivation on the surface of Cdots eliminates the non-radiative self-trapped electrons through removal of other functional groups that alters the PL property. So, there were more chances of excitation-independent PL emission in case of N, S-Cdots as it has high surface passivation due to heteroatom doping as compare to the u-Cdots. Enhancement in the surface passivation on the surface of Cdots eliminates the non-radiative self-trapped electrons through removal of other functional groups that alters the PL property [48].

Fig. S1(c) represents the PL spectra of u-Cdots at different excitation wavelengths i.e., 300 nm to 420 nm. The maximum PL intensity of u-Cdots was found at 475 nm with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (λex). It was found that the shift in PL emission peak of u-Cdots ranged from 430 nm to 530 nm when excited at different wavelengths with a range of 300 nm to 420 nm, respectively. Also, it was observed from the TEM image of u-Cdots that the different size distribution results in the variation of energy band gaps. This shows that the u-Cdots have excitation-dependent PL behavior which may depends upon the quantum confinement effect. Fig. S1(d) represents the PL spectra of N, S-Cdots in which there was no significant shift in the PL emission peak was observed when excited at different wavelengths from 300 to 420 nm. The nearly uniform distribution of particle size was observed in the TEM image of N, S-Cdots. The presence of different surface functional groups results in the introduction of new excitation energy traps and surface defects. Doping also vary the PL behaviour [48]. Nitrogen, sulphur incorporation in the structure of u-Cdots which contain only O-related states results in increase the effect of sulfur and nitrogen through a co-operative effect [24]. This leads to removal of O-related states and increase in N and S-related states, therefore, N, S-doped Cdots exhibit an excitation independent PL emission with high PL quantum yield. Therefore, the excitation-independent PL emission was shown by N, S-Cdots in which surface defects is more dominating factor as compare to the uniform size distribution.

The photostability of prepared carbon dots were examined by measuring the PL intensity at different irradiation times (Fig. S2 (a and b)). It was found that both carbon dots show good photostability but N, S-Cdots show the more photostability as compare to the u-Cdots. High photostability of N, S-Cdots due to presence of many surface defects through heteroatom doping as compare to u-Cdots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots towards Pb2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To check the selectivity of prepared Cdots for the different metal ions, a particular amount of metal ions solution was added to the fixed concentration (0.01 mg/ml) of prepared Cdots. These metal ions are mostly related to the environment such as Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Bi3+, Mg2+, Na+. Fig. 3 (a) displayed the PL intensity of u-Cdots without and with the addition of specific concentrations of different metal ions. In comparison with other metal ions, Pb2+ ions showed a significant enhancement of PL intensity of u-Cdots. This shows that the u-Cdots are highly selective for the Pb2+ ions. It was found that the u-Cdots solution become turbid in presence of Pb2+ ions and this demonstrated the aggregation of u-Cdots. The presence of COOH and OH groups on the surface of u-Cdots showed the interactions with Pb2+ ions lead to aggregation as compared to the other metal ions. Similarly, the selectivity studies for the different metal ions were also performed in the case of prepared N, S-Cdots. In Fig. 3(b), the Pb2+ ions showed the significant PL quenching of N, S-Cdots as compared to the other metal ions. N, S-Cdots contains mostly N-related and O-related functional groups that are electron donor groups which show good binding affinity with Pb2+ ions as compared to other metal ions. Pb2+ ion is considered as heavy metal ion and shows the open shell electronic configuration which result in formation of coordination complex with different surface functional groups present on N, S-Cdots [49]. The nitrogen and sulfur atoms donate the electron pair to the Pb2+ ions via a non-radiative electron transfer and result in the formation of a complex [50]. Pb2+ ions significantly reduce the fluorescence by facilitating the recombination of electron present in conduction band and hole present in valence band [51]. This is because of relativistic contraction by heavy metal ion i.e., Pb2+ and which will enhance the tendency of valence electrons towards the nucleus of elements [46].

The lead ion detection was carried out by two types of prepared Cdots i.e., u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots as a sensing platform under the optimized conditions. Fig. 3(c) represents the PL intensity of u-Cdots (with 0.01 mg/ml concentration) get increased with the addition of different concentrations of Pb2+ ions from 0.1 µM to 1.6 µM. A good linear plot was observed between the values of (F-F0)/F0 and different concentration of Pb2+ ions from 0.1 µM to 1.6 µM (Fig. 3(d)) and 0.00 µM to 0.30 µM (inset image of Fig. 3(d)). Fig. 3(d) and its inset image displays the calibration curves along with linear regression equations i.e., (F-F0)/F0 = 4.737 [Pb2+] + 0.127, and (F-F0)/F0 = 2.986 [Pb2+] + 0.017, respectively. F0 and F refer to the PL intensity of the u-Cdots with and without the addition of Pb2+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 30 nM for the detection of Pb2+ ions.

In the case of N, S-Cdots, Fig. 4 (a) displays the fluorescence intensity of N, S-Cdots (with 0.01 mg/ml concentration) get quenched in the presence of a different concentration of Pb2+ ions ranging from 0.1 µM to 1.6 µM. Fig. 4(b) represents a good linear plot between the PL responses (F-F0)/F0 and different concertation of Pb2+ ions ranging from 0.1 µM to 1.0 µM and 0.00 µM to 0.30 µM (inset image of Fig. 4(b)). Fig. 4(b) and its inset image displays the calibration curves having linear regression equations i.e., (F0-F)/F0 = 0.812 [Pb2+] + 0.041, and (F0-F)/F0 = 1.159 [Pb2+] + 0.036, respectively. F0 and F refer to the PL intensity of the N, S-Cdots with and without the addition of Pb2+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 7 nM for the detection of Pb2+ ions. Table 1 shows the comparison table including various fluorescent probes based on the different methods with the values of LOD and detection range. Some of these methods have an expensive fluorescent probe, less effective detection range, long synthesis process, expensive equipment, etc. The prepared u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots showed effective detection range, cost-effective, short synthesis process, simple, fast, and significant values of LOD. N, S-Cdots showed the best detection limit in comparison with u-Cdots.

Aggregation-induced emission luminogens(AIEgens) show less intense PL emission in the dissolved state whereas it shows highly intense PL emission in an aggregated or solid-state [58]. This exclusive property of AIEgens leads to develop a novel sensor based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). Although, u-Cdots show good PL emission still the addition of Pb2+ ions result in enhancement of the PL emission. In this phenomenon, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of u-Cdots act as recognition units for the detection of Pb2+ ions. These groups lead to form the coordination complex with Pb2+ ions and result in the development of the aggregated state. Therefore, the addition of Pb2+ ions in the u-Cdots result in the increase of PL intensity of u-Cdots based on AIEE. The absorption spectra of u-Cdots exhibited two peaks at 290 nm and 340 nm which get enhanced by increasing the concentration of Pb2+ ions in the u-Cdots (Fig. S3). This may be due to the formation of the coordination complex between u-Cdots-Pb2+ ions leading to aggregation. The TEM image of u-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions can clearly show the aggregation of u-Cdots (Fig. 4(c)). The intramolecular movements get limited in the aggregated state. This results in the reduction of non-radiative decay and enhances the radiative decay and further, leads to stronger PL emission [29]. The average lifetime decay of both prepared Cdots i.e., u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots was estimated (Fig. S4). The values of PL quantum yield and lifetime for u-Cdots and u-Cdots + Pb2+ ions were tabulated in Table S1. Also, the values of radiative (kr ) and non-radiative rate constants (knr ) were evaluated by using the following equations [59]:
(2)
k r
=
ϕ f
τ f
(3)
1
τ f
=
k r
+
k
n r
where, Φ and τ denote the PL QY and lifetime, respectively. The average lifetime of u-Cdots get increased from 5.26 ns to 7.99 ns in the presence of Pb2+ ions (Fig. 4(f)). The values of kr and knr were evaluated by using above equations. It was found that the radiative constants for u-Cdots was get enhanced after the addition of Pb2+ ions along with the reduction of non-radiative constants (Table S1). This clearly demonstrated the PL intensity of u-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ion is based on the AIEE mechanism.

On the other hand, the PL intensity of N, S-Cdots was get quenched with the addition of Pb2+ ions. The PL QY value of N, S-Cdots was get decreased from 0.714 to 0.047 (Table S1). N, S-Cdots contains the different kinds of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, thiol, amine, etc. were confirmed by FTIR and XPS analysis. The interaction of Pb2+ ions with above surface functional groups of N, S-Cdots result in the variation of its PL properties. In dynamic quenching, the complex formation occurs between the Cdots and quencher with a transfer of energy in the excited state along with no change in the UV–visible absorption spectra of the fluorescent probe and also, variation in the lifetime decay with the addition of an analyte [60,61]. It was found that there was no significant change in the UV–vis spectra of N, S-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions (Fig. S5). Fig. 4(e) represents the TEM image of N, S-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions which shows the collision between the N, S-Cdots and Pb2+ ions. Also, the average lifetime of N, S-Cdots was get decreased from 7.75 ns to 2.21 ns with the addition of Pb2+ ions (Fig. 4(d)). Further, it was also observed that the decrease in the radiative constant and increase in non-radiative constants of N, S-Cdots in the presence of Pb2+ ions. This demonstrated that the reduction in the PL intensity of N, S-Cdots was attributed to the dynamic quenching due to the occurrence of a charge transfer between the surface functional groups present on the Cdots and Pb2+ ions in the excited state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ ions in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The developed u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots were used as a sensor for the detection of Pb2+ ions in the real sample i.e., water present in the batteries. A particular amount of real sample was added to both carbon dots. The amount of Pb2+ ions detected by atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), amount of Pb2+ ions detected by the prepared system, and relative standard deviation (RSD) (n = 3) are summarized in Table S2 and which shows that the experiment has given satisfactory results for practical applications. These results demonstrated that these prepared PL sensors are applicable for real sample analysis.

Both the prepared u-Cdots and N, S-Cdots showed high fluorescence, excellent water solubility, and good photostability. But the N, S-Cdots showed high PL intensity and photostability as compare to u-Cdots due to heteroatom doping which provides a huge number of active sites and the synergistic effect between each element leads to radiative recombination of the trapped electron-holes pairs. As the surface passivation increases on the surface of Cdots, the non-radiative self-trapped electrons get eliminated by removal of other surface functional groups that change the PL property. So, the N, S-Cdots shows excitation-independent PL emission as it has high surface passivation due to heteroatom doping in comparison with the u-Cdots. The addition of Pb2+ ions in both carbon dots exhibits the opposite result i.e., “Turn-on” of PL intensity for u-Cdots and “Turn-off” of PL intensity for N, S-Cdots based on different mechanisms i.e., AIEE and dynamic quenching, respectively. These mechanisms were verified by UV–vis absorption spectra, TEM, PL quantum yield, and lifetime decay studies. N, S-Cdots shows best LOD value as compare to u-Cdots. The prepared sensing probes also have been used for real sample detection.
Neeraj Sohal: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Soumen Basu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Banibrata Maity: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Neeraj Sohal is grateful to CSIR, India (Grant no: 01 (2955)/18/ EMR-II) for a fellowship and funding. Dr. Banibrata Maity is also thankful to Seed Grant Project, TIET, Patiala for research funding. The authors acknowledge IIT, Roorkee for the XPS study and also for the TEM facility, funded by a TPF Nanomission, GOI project at Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences, Bengaluru. The authors are grateful to Dr. Nimai Mishra, SRM University, Amaravati, for lifetime analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108287.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
